Paper 1


SME Mark Scheme — Paper 1 Hard


Use the question-specific mark scheme together with the mark bands. Award up to the maximum
marks as indicated.

1. (a) Using appropriate diagrams, explain how negative production externalities lead to market
failure and a welfare loss. [10]

Answers may include:
      • Terminology: Market failure, externality, MPC, MSC, MPB, MSB, welfare (dead-weight) loss,
      social optimum, allocative efficiency.
      • Diagram: A negative externality of production diagram with MSC > MPC, the market equilibrium
      at Qm > Q* and a clearly shaded welfare-loss triangle.
      • Theory: An explanation that when private costs are less than social costs the market
      over-produces, allocating too many resources to the good. The welfare loss measures the lost
      benefit to society from the over-consumed units.

Assessment Criteria — Part (a) 10 marks

  Marks          Level descriptor

  0              The work does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below.

  1–2            The response indicates little understanding of the specific demands of the question.
                 Economic theory is stated but it is not relevant.
                 Economic terms are stated but they are not relevant.

  3–4            The response indicates some understanding of the specific demands of the
                 question.
                 Relevant economic theory is described.
                 Some relevant economic terms are included.

  5–6            The response indicates understanding of the specific demands of the question, but
                 these demands are only partially addressed.
                 Relevant economic theory is partly explained.
                 Some relevant economic terms are used appropriately.
                 Where appropriate, relevant diagram(s) are included.

  7–8            The specific demands of the question are understood and addressed.
                 Relevant economic theory is explained.
                 Relevant economic terms are used mostly appropriately.
                 Where appropriate, relevant diagram(s) are included and explained.


                                                   1                                    SME Predicted Paper
                                                                    SME Mark Scheme P1 — Hard


9–10   The specific demands of the question are understood and addressed.
       Relevant economic theory is fully explained.
       Relevant economic terms are used appropriately throughout the response.
       Where appropriate, relevant diagram(s) are included and fully explained.

                                                                                  SME Mark Scheme P1 — Hard


1. (b) Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that carbon taxes are the most effective policy to
correct market failure caused by pollution. [15]

Answers may include:
      • Terminology: Carbon tax, Pigouvian tax, tradable permits, emissions trading scheme (ETS),
      regulation, subsidies on renewables, negative externality.
      • Diagram: Negative externality of production diagram showing a per-unit tax shifting MPC up to
      internalise the externality.
      • Theory: A carbon tax raises producer costs, shifts supply left, raises price and reduces polluting
      output. It generates revenue for green investment.
      • Synthesis (evaluate): Hard to set the 'correct' tax given uncertain valuation of damages.
      Regressive effects on low-income households. Trade-exposed industries may relocate (carbon
      leakage). Alternatives include cap-and-trade (certainty on quantity, price volatile), regulation
      (effective but inflexible), and subsidies for clean tech. A policy mix often works best.
      • Example(s): Sweden’s carbon tax, EU ETS, British Columbia’s revenue-neutral carbon tax, the
      US Inflation Reduction Act’s subsidies.

Assessment Criteria — Part (b) 15 marks

  Marks           Level descriptor

  0               The work does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below.

                  Economic theory is stated but it is not relevant.
                  Economic terms are stated but they are not relevant.

                  A real-world example(s) is identified but it is irrelevant.

                  question.

2. (a) Explain, using an AD/AS diagram, the difference between demand-pull inflation and cost-push
inflation. [10]

Answers may include:
      • Terminology: Demand-pull inflation, cost-push inflation, aggregate demand, aggregate supply,
      SRAS, LRAS, average price level.
      • Diagrams: Two AD/AS diagrams — one with AD shifting right (demand-pull), another with
      SRAS shifting left (cost-push). Labels must be complete.
      • Theory: Demand-pull arises from excess AD (e.g. consumer boom, fiscal/monetary stimulus).
      Cost-push arises from rising production costs (oil shocks, wage push, weaker currency raising
      import prices).

2. (b) Using real-world examples, discuss the view that contractionary monetary policy is always
more effective than contractionary fiscal policy in reducing a high rate of inflation. [15]

Answers may include:
      • Terminology: Central-bank independence, policy rate, money supply, fiscal consolidation,
      automatic stabilisers, crowding out.
      • Diagram: AD/AS diagram showing a leftward shift in AD reducing the price level.
      • Theory: Higher interest rates reduce C and I; stronger currency reduces import prices. Tighter
      fiscal policy (higher taxes, lower G) reduces AD directly.
      • Synthesis (evaluate): Monetary policy is quicker to deploy and politically easier, but blunt and
      effective mainly on demand-pull inflation. Fiscal policy can target specific sources of inflation (e.g.
      VAT cuts on energy to reduce cost-push), but suffers from political and time lags. Supply-side
      factors (e.g. oil shocks) may require non-AD policies. The best response typically combines tools.
      • Example(s): Fed rate hikes 2022–23, ECB tightening, UK fiscal energy subsidies 2022–23,
      Turkey’s unconventional rate cuts and resulting inflation.

3. (a) Using a diagram, explain how a tariff imposed on imported goods affects domestic producers,
consumers and the government. [10]

Answers may include:
      • Terminology: Tariff, world price, domestic supply/demand, consumer surplus, producer
      surplus, government revenue, dead-weight loss.
      • Diagram: A full tariff diagram with world supply, world supply plus tariff, and clearly labelled
      areas for consumer-surplus loss, producer-surplus gain, government revenue and welfare loss.
      • Theory: Consumers lose surplus, domestic producers gain surplus, government gains revenue,
      but the two welfare-loss triangles mean society is worse off overall.

3. (b) Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that free trade always benefits developing
economies more than it benefits developed economies. [15]

Answers may include:
      • Terminology: Comparative advantage, free trade, terms of trade, primary product dependency,
      infant industry, sunrise industry, trade liberalisation, Prebisch–Singer.
      • Diagram: Consumer/producer surplus analysis of moving from autarky/tariff to free trade; or a
      PPC shifting outward.
      • Theory: Free trade allows developing countries to exploit comparative advantage, access larger
      markets, attract FDI, import technology, and reduce poverty.
      • Synthesis (evaluate): Developed economies can benefit at least as much: cheap imports, new
      export markets, consumer welfare, and strong negotiating leverage that yields favourable terms
      of trade. Developing countries can be locked into low-value primary exports, suffer from declining
      terms of trade, and struggle to develop sunrise industries without protection.
      • Example(s): China’s post-WTO growth (developing), US/EU bargaining power in trade deals,
      Vietnam’s CPTPP benefits, sub-Saharan African commodity exporters.
